task: Observe flow of materials and listen for machine malfunctions, such as jamming or spillage, and notify supervisors if corrective actions fail.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring patrol/shift segment; a competent worker continuously observes material flow and listens for malfunctions, reporting issues roughly every 30-minute cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Current humanoids can monitor via cameras/mics and detect obvious anomalies, but reliably distinguishing subtle jamming sounds, assessing spillage severity, and escalating to supervisors through workplace communication systems remain significant challenges.

task: Lay out and fabricate metal structural parts such as plates, bulkheads, and frames.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out and fabricating a single structural metal part (e.g., a frame or bulkhead). A competent metalworker typically takes 1–3 hours depending on complexity; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise layout/measurement, operating grinders, plasma cutters, welders, and bending equipment on large heavy stock. No current general-purpose humanoid has the dexterity, tool-use breadth, or planning to execute this fabrication workflow autonomously at acceptable quality.

task: Set up, operate, or tend plating or coating machines to coat metal or plastic products with chromium, zinc, copper, cadmium, nickel, or other metal to protect or decorate surfaces.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and running a single plating/coating batch. Includes machine setup, loading parts, monitoring process, and unloading. Typical batch cycle is 30–60 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial chemical machinery, monitoring bath chemistry, adjusting parameters, and handling hazardous liquids. Current humanoids lack the tool interfacing, process sensing, and chemical handling capability for this complex multi-step industrial workflow.

task: Monitor machine operations to determine whether adjustments are necessary, stopping machines when problems occur.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring cycle on one machine: observe, assess status, decide to adjust or stop. Operators typically do this every 15–30 min per machine.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can use cameras/sensors for basic anomaly detection, but interpreting subtle machine states, making nuanced adjustment decisions, and physically intervening (e.g., controls, emergency stops) in unstructured factory environments remains unreliable for general-purpose humanoids.

task: Operate and maintain laser plate-making equipment that converts electronic data to plates without the use of film.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing one plate job: loading media, configuring settings from electronic data, running the laser platesetter, inspecting output, and performing routine maintenance checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating highly specialized CTP (computer-to-plate) industrial equipment with complex digital interfaces, fine media handling, software configuration, and quality inspection. Current humanoid robots lack the software integration, specialized tool-use, and domain perception needed.

task: Inspect track for defects such as broken rails and switch malfunctions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a ~1-mile track segment. Inspector walks/rides checking rails, joints, fasteners, and switches; ~60 min is a standard unit of measurement.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locomotion over uneven ballast/rail terrain (poor for humanoids), specialized perception (ultrasonic, visual crack detection), and switch function testing. Outdoor unstructured environment with safety-critical accuracy demands far exceeds current robot generalization.

task: Test and charge batteries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one battery. Connecting to tester, reading output, attaching to charger, and confirming charge cycle started takes ~20 min including brief monitoring.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise connector alignment, reading displays/meters, and interpreting variable battery types. Perception and fine dexterity for small terminals and varied connectors is challenging; generalization across battery types further limits success.

task: Report any unusual circumstances concerning mail delivery, including the condition of street letter boxes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = reporting on one mail route segment, including walking the route, observing mailboxes, and logging/noting any anomalies. Typical urban block takes ~8 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor locomotion on uneven sidewalks (~40%), reading handwritten/printed labels, assessing physical condition of varied mailbox types, and generating a coherent report—all combined push success low.

task: Provide assistance to workers engaged in construction or extraction activities, using hand tools or other equipment.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one shift's worth of assistance tasks (fetching tools, moving materials, holding components). A competent helper completes a meaningful unit of support work in ~45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Outdoor/uneven terrain, unstructured environments, heavy/bulky tool handling, and real-time coordination with human workers are major barriers. Current humanoids lack robust outdoor mobility and generalized tool-use for jobsite chaos.

task: Assemble and cement sections of stained glass together.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one standard stained glass panel (~30–50 pieces). A skilled glazier spends ~2 hours cutting, fitting, leading/soldering, and cementing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity to handle fragile, irregular glass shards, precise lead/solder work, and force-sensitive cementing. Far beyond current humanoid robot manipulation capabilities; high breakage and alignment failure risk.

task: Remove fixtures such as pictures, door knobs, lamps, or electric switch covers prior to painting.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing all fixtures in a typical room (8–12 items: switch covers, door knobs, picture hooks, lamps). A competent painter takes ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (small screws, varied fasteners), tool use (screwdrivers), and handling fragile/irregular items. Current humanoids struggle with diverse fastener types, awkward angles, and delicate fixtures reliably.

task: Remove asbestos or lead from surfaces, using hand or power tools such as scrapers, vacuums, or high-pressure sprayers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = removing asbestos/lead from a single room or surface area (~100 sq ft). A certified abatement worker typically takes 1–3 hours including setup, scraping/spraying, and containment.
robot_success_rationale: Requires hazmat PPE donning, navigating confined/cluttered spaces, applying variable force with scrapers/sprayers on irregular surfaces, and strict containment protocols. Current humanoids lack hazmat compliance, fine surface-tracking, and adaptive tool-force control for abatement work.

task: Assemble and install communication equipment such as data and telephone communication lines, wiring, switching equipment, wiring frames, power apparatus, computer systems, and networks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single communication equipment installation job (e.g., running data/telephone lines, mounting switching gear, and connecting a small network). A competent telecom technician typically takes 3–5 hours per site or job unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires routing cables through walls/conduits, crimping connectors, mounting rack equipment, and configuring networks — combining fine dexterity, unstructured environments, tool use (drills, crimpers, testers), and domain-specific judgment far beyond current humanoid capabilities.

task: Clean switches and replace contact points, using vacuum hoses, solvents, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and replacing contact points on a single switch assembly. A competent technician spends ~20–40 min accessing, vacuuming, applying solvent, and swapping contact points.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterous manipulation (small contact points, hand tools, solvents), tool switching, and inspection judgment. Far beyond current humanoid dexterity and tool-use generalization; sub-5% probability.

task: Test battery charges, and replace or recharge batteries as necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one battery and deciding to replace/recharge. Attaching a tester, reading the result, and swapping/recharging takes ~5 minutes for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise connector attachment, reading a multimeter/tester display, interpreting results, then conditionally replacing or recharging—multi-step tool use, fine dexterity with varied connectors, and conditional decision-making exceed current humanoid capabilities reliably.

task: Lubricate wearing surfaces of mechanical parts, using oils or other lubricants.
human_time_rationale: One instance = lubricating a single component or set of wearing surfaces (e.g., a bearing, joint, or chain). A skilled technician locates, accesses, and applies lubricant in ~5–10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires locating specific lubrication points, handling oil cans/grease guns, and applying precise amounts. Perception of small fittings, tool manipulation, and access in tight spaces challenge current humanoids significantly.

task: Trace and tag meters or house lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = tracing and tagging one meter or house line. A competent utility worker typically takes 20–40 min to locate, trace the run, and apply a tag.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating cluttered utility spaces, visually tracing wires/pipes across complex environments, reading labels, and applying physical tags. Current robots lack reliable wire-tracing perception, fine tagging dexterity, and generalization in unstructured utility settings.

task: Vary air pressure flowing into regulators and turn handles to assess functioning of valves and pistons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing one valve/piston unit: connecting air supply, varying pressure via regulator, turning handles, and observing response. A competent technician completes this in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise handle manipulation, pressure regulation via turn-knobs, and functional assessment/judgment. Current humanoids lack fine dexterity for valve handles, reliable tool-use for gauges, and the perceptual reasoning to assess piston/valve behavior under varying pressure.

task: Cut, strip, and bend wire leads at ends of coils, using pliers and wire scrapers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing the wire leads on one coil (cut, strip, and bend both ends). A competent worker completes this in ~3 minutes using hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine dexterity: gripping thin wire leads, applying calibrated force with pliers/scrapers, bending to spec without damaging coil. Current humanoids lack the finger-level precision and force control needed reliably.

task: Tend machines that bond cope and drag together to form completed shell molds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = bonding one cope-and-drag pair into a completed shell mold. A competent foundry worker loads, aligns, activates the bonding machine, and unloads in ~5 minutes per mold.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise alignment of fragile shell mold halves, machine tending with industrial controls, and quality inspection. Perception of mold seam alignment and handling brittle parts without breakage challenge current humanoids; structured environment helps slightly.

task: Select lumber to be used for patterns.
human_time_rationale: One instance = selecting lumber pieces for a single pattern batch. A skilled worker visually inspects, grades, and picks suitable boards in ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced visual/tactile judgment of grain, knots, warping, and moisture suitability. Current robots lack reliable fine-grained wood quality assessment and the expert selection logic needed for pattern-making standards.

task: Tighten pulleys or add abrasives to maintain cutting speeds.
human_time_rationale: One instance = adjusting one pulley or dressing/adding abrasive to one machine. A skilled operator diagnoses, adjusts, and verifies in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires diagnosing cutting speed deviation, applying precise torque to pulley tensioners, and handling abrasive materials — all demanding fine dexterity, force feedback, and domain-specific judgment beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Grade, classify, or sort products according to sizes, weights, colors, or other specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = grading/sorting one item (e.g., one piece of produce, one part). A competent worker handles ~30–60 items/hour depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Vision-based classification is mature; robots can sort by color, size, and weight with sensors. Challenges remain with irregular/deformable items, fine color gradients, and graceful handling to avoid damage.

task: Clean semiconductor wafers using cleaning equipment, such as chemical baths, automatic wafer cleaners, or blow-off wands.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning a batch of wafers through a full cleaning cycle (load, run chemical bath or auto-cleaner, dry/blow-off, unload). Typical cycle is 15–25 minutes for a competent technician.
robot_success_rationale: Wafers are extremely fragile, thin, and require ultra-precise, contamination-free handling. Current humanoid robots lack the sub-millimeter dexterity, cleanroom compliance, and specialized tool-use needed to handle wafers safely and autonomously.

task: Place products in containers according to grade and mark grades on containers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sorting and placing a batch of products into graded containers and marking them. A competent worker handles this quickly, ~3 min per batch/container set.
robot_success_rationale: Requires grading/inspection perception, sorting by grade, placing into correct containers, AND marking/labeling. Chained multi-step task with perception, dexterity (writing/labeling), and decision-making; current robots struggle with full autonomy across all steps.

task: Fit and fasten wallboard or drywall into position on wood or metal frameworks, using glue, nails, or screws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing one standard 4×8 ft drywall sheet: measuring/cutting, positioning, and fastening to framing. A competent drywaller takes ~30–60 min per sheet including layout.
robot_success_rationale: Requires lifting and precisely positioning a large, heavy, fragile panel overhead or flush to framing, then fastening with screws — demanding bimanual coordination, whole-body force control, spatial reasoning in cluttered sites, and tool use. Far beyond current humanoid capability.

task: Move or position materials such as marble slabs, using cranes, hoists, or dollies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = moving/positioning a single marble slab using equipment. Includes rigging, operating crane/hoist/dolly, and placing the slab; ~20–30 min for a skilled worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized heavy equipment (cranes, hoists, dollies) that humanoid robots cannot drive or control. Rigging heavy, irregular stone slabs demands high-force dexterity and safety judgment far beyond 2026 frontier robots.

task: Observe leveling indicator arms to verify levelness and alignment of tracks.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking leveling indicator arms along one track section. A competent worker visually observes and interprets indicators in ~2–4 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires visual perception of indicator arms and interpreting subtle alignment cues. Modern robots have decent vision but struggle with precise gauge reading, cluttered industrial environments, and reliably navigating track-side terrain.

task: Perform pumping tests to assess well performance.
human_time_rationale: One pumping test instance covers setup, active pumping (typically 1–4 hours), and initial data recording for a single well. ~240 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating heavy pump equipment, connecting hoses/gauges, managing field instrumentation, and interpreting hydrogeological data outdoors on uneven terrain. Far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities in autonomy and tool-use complexity.

task: Clean and prepare sites for excavation or boring.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing a single excavation/boring site: clearing debris, marking boundaries, removing surface obstacles, and staging equipment. Typical crew completes this in ~60–120 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough terrain navigation, heavy debris removal, operating clearing equipment (shovels, skid steers), and contextual judgment about site hazards. Current humanoids lack equipment operation, outdoor terrain robustness, and heavy-lifting capacity needed.

task: Test and replace electrical components and wiring, using test meters, soldering equipment, and hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = diagnosing, removing, and replacing a single faulty electrical component/wiring segment. A competent electrician/technician takes ~30–60 min including testing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous tool use (multimeter probes, soldering iron, wire strippers), fault diagnosis reasoning, precise connector manipulation, and safe electrical handling. Far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured settings.

task: Observe and test operation of appliances following installation, and make any initial installation adjustments that are necessary.
human_time_rationale: One instance = observing and test-running one newly installed appliance (e.g. washer, oven, HVAC unit) and making initial adjustments; typical technician takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting appliance behavior, reading displays/error codes, making fine mechanical/electrical adjustments, and exercising trade judgment. Current robots lack the sensory interpretation, tool dexterity, and generalization needed for varied appliances and adjustment types.

task: Inspect and test docks, ships, buoyage systems, plant intakes or outflows, or underwater pipelines, cables, or sewers, using closed circuit television, still photography, and testing equipment.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance covers a single structure/asset (e.g., one dock or pipeline segment). A competent commercial diver/inspector typically takes 2–4 hours including setup, survey, photography, and equipment pack-down.
robot_success_rationale: Requires underwater mobility, waterproofing, CCTV/photography tool-use, navigation in turbid/unstructured aquatic environments, and multi-modal inspection across vastly different asset types. Current humanoid robots are not rated for underwater deployment; specialized underwater ROVs exist but are not general-purpose humanoids. Autonomous quality acceptable to a supervisor remains far out of reach.

task: Cut and weld steel, using underwater welding equipment, jigs, and supports.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single underwater cut-and-weld job on a steel structure. A certified diver-welder typically spends 60–120 min per joint/cut, including setup of jigs and supports.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating underwater in unstructured aquatic environments, handling high-voltage welding equipment, precise torch control, and positioning jigs — far beyond any current general-purpose humanoid robot's locomotion, perception, or dexterity in submerged conditions.

task: Mask off mold areas not to be laminated, using cellophane, wax paper, masking tape, or special sprays containing mold-release substances.
human_time_rationale: One instance = masking off a single mold. A competent worker inspects the mold, selects materials (tape, wax paper, spray), and applies masking carefully around boundaries; ~15–25 min typical.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to apply tape/film precisely along irregular mold edges, interpret which zones to mask, and use spray cans. Current humanoids lack the precision, compliance sensing, and task understanding for acceptable masking quality.

task: Unwind lengths of yarn, thread, or twine from spools and wind onto bobbins.
human_time_rationale: One instance = winding one bobbin from a spool. A competent worker threads, winds, and cuts in ~5–10 minutes depending on length and material.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity to thread filament onto a bobbin core, precise tension control throughout winding, and handling of flexible/slippery materials — all well beyond reliable 2026 humanoid capability.

task: Fit, install, and secure material on frames, using hand tools, power tools, glue, cement, or staples.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting and securing material on one frame. A skilled worker takes ~30–60 min depending on material type, frame size, and fastening method.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-step dexterity: stretching/aligning flexible material, switching tools (staple gun, glue, cement), applying correct tension, and navigating varied frame geometries. Far beyond reliable autonomous robot capability in 2026.

task: Perform tests to check the qualities and grades of products, such as assessing levels of bottom sediment, water, and foreign materials in oil samples, using centrifugal testers.
human_time_rationale: One instance = testing a single oil sample via centrifugal tester: preparing the sample, loading it, running the centrifuge cycle (~10–15 min), and reading/recording results.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation (pipetting, handling glass tubes, precise loading into centrifuge), reading analog/digital lab instruments, and interpreting graded results — well beyond reliable autonomous robot capability in 2026.

task: Count or weigh and bundle items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = counting/weighing and bundling a single batch of items (e.g., a stack of papers, a bundle of rebar, or a bag of produce). A competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can pick and place items, but reliably counting, weighing (requires tool integration), and then neatly bundling with bands/ties demands fine dexterity, tool use, and generalization across varied item types — currently error-prone.

task: Mark cutting lines around patterns or templates, or follow layout points, using squares, rules, and straightedges, and chalk, pencils, or scribes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = marking a single pattern/template layout on material. A skilled worker positions the template, aligns guides, and scribes all cutting lines in ~10–20 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor control to scribe accurate lines, spatial reasoning to align templates and follow layout points, and tool manipulation (chalk/pencil/scribe + straightedge). Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter dexterity and coordinated bimanual precision this demands.

task: Unroll, lay out, attach, or mount materials or items on cutting tables or machines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = unrolling/laying out one section of material on a cutting table or machine. A skilled worker typically takes 5–10 minutes depending on material size and alignment needs.
robot_success_rationale: Handling large, flexible, or rolled materials (fabric, film, sheet goods) is highly challenging for robots — requires bimanual coordination, deformable-object manipulation, precise alignment, and tension control. Current humanoids lack reliable capability for this.

task: Perform general labor duties such as repairing booms.
human_time_rationale: One instance = repairing a single boom (e.g., crane/agricultural/oil-containment). A competent laborer typically spends ~1 hour inspecting, disassembling, fixing, and reassembling.
robot_success_rationale: Boom repair requires heavy tool use, varied fasteners, structural force application, and high situational judgment in unstructured outdoor/industrial environments. Current humanoids lack the strength, specialized tool dexterity, and task generalization needed.

task: Operate power-driven pumps that transfer liquids, semi-liquids, gases, or powdered materials.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one pump operation cycle (setup, run, shutdown) for a single transfer job; ~30 min including pre-checks and monitoring.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial controls, reading gauges, adjusting valves, and responding to process feedback. Current humanoids lack robust tool-use for industrial valve/control interfaces, process-sensing integration, and fault response in unstructured plant environments.

task: Assemble or adjust parts or related electrical units of prototypes to prepare for testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling/adjusting one prototype unit. A competent technician typically spends 30–60 min on mechanical fit, electrical connections, and alignment checks before test-readiness.
robot_success_rationale: Prototype assembly involves non-standardized, novel parts with tight tolerances, fine wiring/connector insertion, and judgment-based adjustments. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, adaptive reasoning, and tool-use precision this demands.

task: Provide adapted physical education services to students with intellectual disabilities, autism, traumatic brain injury, orthopedic impairments, or other disabling condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single adapted PE session for a student/small group. Typical sessions run 30–60 min including setup, instruction, adaptation, and wrap-up.
robot_success_rationale: Requires dynamic social-emotional responsiveness, real-time behavioral/safety judgment, individualized instructional adaptation, and trust-building with vulnerable students — far beyond current robot autonomy, communication, or caregiving capability.

task: Diagnose infections, such as Hepatitis B and Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome (AIDS), by conducting tests to detect the antibodies that patients' immune systems make to fight such infections.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full diagnostic workflow for one patient: collecting sample, running antibody assay (e.g., ELISA), interpreting results, and documenting findings. ~45 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, complex lab instrument operation, sample handling with sterile technique, and result interpretation. Far beyond current humanoid robot autonomy; highly regulated, safety-critical domain.

task: Assess birthing environments to ensure cleanliness, safety, and the availability of appropriate supplies.
human_time_rationale: One instance = inspecting a single birthing room/suite; a trained midwife/nurse checks cleanliness, safety hazards, and supply inventory, typically taking 20–30 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, nuanced contamination detection, verifying sterile packaging integrity, and inventory of specialized medical supplies. Current robots lack medical-context perception, domain knowledge, and reliable fine-grained inspection to meet clinical standards.

task: Prepare or serve hot or cold beverages, such as coffee, espresso drinks, blended coffees, or teas.
human_time_rationale: One instance = preparing and serving a single beverage order (e.g., a latte or blended coffee). A skilled barista typically takes 2–6 minutes from order to hand-off.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (tamping, steaming, pouring latte art), operating specialized espresso/blending equipment, handling hot liquids safely, and adapting to varied orders. Current humanoids lack reliable fine manipulation and tool-use for complex barista workflows.

task: Install active solar systems, including solar collectors, concentrators, pumps, or fans.
human_time_rationale: One instance = installing a typical residential active solar system (collectors, concentrators, pumps/fans, piping). A crew of 2 competent installers takes ~4–6 hours total; median ~4 hrs per system.
robot_success_rationale: Requires rooftop mobility on uneven, sloped surfaces, heavy lifting, precise pipe/fitting connections, electrical wiring, and use of specialized tools. Far beyond current humanoid robots' outdoor locomotion, dexterity, and tool-use generalization limits.

task: Apply weather seal, such as pipe flashings and sealants, to roof penetrations and structural devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = sealing one roof penetration (e.g., pipe flashing + sealant bead). A competent roofer takes ~30–60 min including positioning, fitting the flashing, and applying sealant.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rooftop locomotion on sloped, slippery surfaces, precise flashing fitting around irregular penetrations, and controlled sealant application — all well beyond current humanoid dexterity, mobility, and generalization capabilities.

task: Operate equipment to start, stop, or regulate biomass-fueled generators, generator units, boilers, engines, or auxiliary systems.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one start/stop/regulation cycle of a biomass generator or boiler system; a competent operator takes ~15–25 min including checks, adjustments, and logging.
robot_success_rationale: Requires operating specialized industrial control panels, valves, switches, and HMI screens in a harsh, unstructured plant environment. Current humanoids lack the tool-use, plant-floor generalization, and safety-critical reliability needed. No commercial deployment exists.

task: Develop laser-processed designs, such as laser-cut medical devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing a single laser-processed medical device (CAD/CAM design through to validated cut file). A competent engineer typically spends 2–5 hours per design iteration.
robot_success_rationale: Task is primarily cognitive/creative: CAD design, regulatory knowledge, material selection, and iterative testing. Current humanoid robots lack design software proficiency, abstract engineering reasoning, and creative problem-solving needed for this.

task: Set up, operate, or monitor invasive equipment and devices, such as colostomy or tracheotomy equipment, mechanical ventilators, catheters, gastrointestinal tubes, and central lines.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up or performing a single check/adjustment of one invasive device (e.g., ventilator check, catheter insertion, or colostomy bag change). A competent nurse takes ~15–25 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity, sterile technique, anatomical precision, and real-time clinical judgment on a vulnerable patient. Far beyond current humanoid robots' dexterity, safety certification, and adaptive perception capabilities.

task: Obtain specimens or samples for laboratory work.
human_time_rationale: One instance = collecting a single specimen/sample (e.g., blood draw, soil core, swab). A competent technician typically takes 10–20 minutes including prep, collection, and labeling.
robot_success_rationale: Specimen collection spans highly varied, often delicate contexts (venipuncture, swabbing, soil coring). Requires fine dexterity, tactile feedback, sterile technique, and contextual judgment. Current humanoids lack reliable fine dexterous manipulation and sterile handling needed.

task: Transport specimens, laboratory items, or pharmacy items, ensuring proper documentation and delivery to authorized personnel.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pickup, transport, and documented handoff of one item/batch within a facility. Typical run including transit and sign-off averages ~15 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can navigate flat indoor corridors moderately well, but challenges include handling fragile/biohazard specimens, operating elevators, interfacing with access-controlled areas, verifying authorized recipients, and completing documentation/chain-of-custody requirements.

task: Train other medical personnel in phlebotomy or laboratory techniques.
human_time_rationale: One training session covering phlebotomy or lab techniques for a small group; includes demonstration, hands-on practice oversight, and Q&A — typically 1–2 hours per session.
robot_success_rationale: Training requires verbal instruction, adaptive explanation, live demonstration of fine needle/tube techniques, and real-time error correction. Current robots lack conversational pedagogy, fine dexterity for live demo, and autonomous teaching judgment.

task: Inspect completed work to ensure all hardware is tight, antennas are level, hangers are properly fastened, proper support is in place, or adequate weather proofing has been installed.
human_time_rationale: One inspection instance covers a completed installation (e.g., a single antenna/mount site). A competent technician visually checks and physically tests all elements in ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires multi-modal inspection: visual levelness, torque-feel on hardware, weatherproofing integrity, and working at height/on masts. Current robots lack reliable torque-sensing, elevation access, and judgment for pass/fail quality standards in unstructured field environments.

task: Perform regular maintenance of laboratory equipment by inspecting, calibrating, cleaning, or sterilizing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full maintenance cycle (inspect, calibrate, clean/sterilize) on one piece of lab equipment. Competent technician typically takes 30–60 min depending on complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity for calibration instruments, domain-specific tool use, reading equipment displays, interpreting results, and applying sterilization protocols — well beyond current general-purpose robot capabilities in unstructured lab settings.

task: Measure or weigh ingredients used in laboratory testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring/weighing a single ingredient or reagent for a lab procedure. A competent lab technician typically takes 3–7 minutes including taring, dispensing, and recording.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (pipettes, spatulas, small vials), precise force control, reading digital scales/graduations, and handling liquids/powders safely. Current humanoids lack the sub-millimeter precision and reliable tool use needed for lab-quality accuracy.

task: Assist mechanics and perform various mechanical duties, such as changing oil or checking and replacing batteries.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single service task (e.g., one oil change or battery swap). A competent mechanic's helper completes this in ~15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating under vehicles, operating pneumatic tools, handling fluids, and fine-fitting battery terminals in tight, variable engine-bay spaces. Current humanoids lack the tool-use, force control, and spatial adaptability for automotive underbody work.

task: Assist in bus loading and unloading.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assisting passengers on/off one bus (one stop or boarding/alighting cycle). A human aide guides passengers, handles luggage, and manages mobility aids in ~10 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating crowded, dynamic outdoor environments, assisting diverse passengers (elderly, disabled, luggage), operating ramps/lifts, and handling unpredictable social interactions — far beyond current humanoid generalization and caregiving capability.

task: Sweep or vacuum the cremation chamber to retrieve remains for storage in an urn or other container.
human_time_rationale: One instance = retrieving all remains from one cremation chamber. A trained operator sweeps, collects, and transfers remains to a container, typically taking 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous sweeping of fragile ash/bone fragments inside a confined, irregularly-shaped high-temperature chamber, precise collection without loss, and careful transfer to a container. Current robots lack the delicate tool control, confined-space mobility, and complete retrieval reliability needed.

task: Measure and mark cutting lines on materials, using a ruler, pencil, chalk, and marking gauge.
human_time_rationale: One instance = measuring and marking a single set of cut lines on one workpiece. A competent worker typically completes this in ~5 minutes including tool selection, measurement, and marking.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise bimanual coordination: holding a ruler steady while scribing accurate lines with a pencil/chalk/gauge. Fine dexterity, controlled pressure, and sub-millimeter spatial accuracy exceed current humanoid robot capabilities reliably.

task: Verify trueness of structure, using plumb bob and level.
human_time_rationale: One instance = verifying one structural element (wall, post, or frame). Worker sets up plumb bob/level, takes readings, and notes results in ~15 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robot must handle a plumb bob (fine dexterity, string management) or hold a level precisely against surfaces, read analog/digital outputs, and interpret results. Tool handling and reliable interpretation in unstructured construction sites remain major limitations.

task: Guide and stop cars by switching, applying brakes, or placing scotches, or wooden wedges, between wheels and rails.
human_time_rationale: One instance = guiding/stopping one rail car by applying brakes, switching, or placing scotches. A competent worker completes this in ~5 minutes including positioning and securing.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor/uneven terrain navigation, heavy tool use, precise placement of wedges under moving rail cars, and real-time safety-critical judgment. Current humanoids lack the dexterity, force capacity, and situational awareness for this reliably.

task: Connect and turn regulator valves to activate and adjust gas flow and pressure so that desired flames are obtained.
human_time_rationale: One instance = connecting a regulator valve, turning it to open/adjust gas flow, and confirming desired flame. A competent worker does this in ~5 minutes including safety checks.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of threaded fittings and small valves, sensory feedback (flame quality, pressure gauges), and safety-critical gas handling. Current humanoids lack reliable fine-fingered torque control and gas-hazard awareness for autonomous operation.

task: Inspect locomotives to detect damaged or worn parts.
human_time_rationale: One instance = full inspection of one locomotive. A competent rail technician typically takes ~45–90 minutes to walk around, check mechanical, electrical, and brake components systematically.
robot_success_rationale: Locomotives are massive, complex, and in unstructured rail yard environments. Robots lack the mobility to access undercarriages, the specialized perception for rail-specific faults, and the generalization to interpret subtle wear across dozens of varied component types.

task: Examine patients to diagnose oral health conditions and diseases.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient oral exam. A competent dentist spends ~20–40 min on a comprehensive diagnostic exam including history review, visual inspection, probing, and X-ray review.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine intraoral dexterity, clinical reasoning, patient communication, and diagnostic judgment. Current humanoids lack the precision tools, certified clinical AI integration, and safe patient interaction needed for autonomous diagnosis.

task: Count money in cash drawers at the beginning of shifts to ensure that amounts are correct and that there is adequate change.
human_time_rationale: One instance = counting a single cash drawer at shift start. A competent cashier counts and verifies all denominations in ~3–7 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterous manipulation of paper bills and coins, recognition of denominations, accurate counting, and logging results. Current robots lack reliable currency handling dexterity and the precise tactile feedback needed to separate and count mixed bills/coins accurately.

task: Prime and paint repaired surfaces, using paint sprayguns and motorized sanders.
human_time_rationale: One instance = priming and painting one repaired surface panel (e.g., auto body or wall section). Includes sanding, masking, priming, and spray painting — ~30–60 min for a competent worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires coordinated use of specialized power tools (sprayer, sander), precise distance/angle control, masking, and quality judgment on finish. Current humanoids lack reliable tool-use generalization, spray control, and surface-quality perception for acceptable results.

task: Fit and secure windows, vinyl roofs, and metal trim to vehicle bodies, using caulking guns, adhesive brushes, and mallets.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fitting and securing all windows, vinyl roof, and trim on one vehicle body. A skilled auto body worker takes ~30–60 min depending on vehicle complexity.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled insertion of large, heavy panels with adhesive/caulk, mallet strikes, and fine trim alignment on curved surfaces. Current humanoids lack the bimanual strength, precision, and tactile feedback needed for consistent automotive-quality results.

task: Fabricate or repair counters, benches, partitions, or other wooden structures, such as sheds or outbuildings.
human_time_rationale: One instance = fabricating or repairing a single structure (e.g., a workbench or shed wall section). A competent carpenter typically takes 3–5 hours per unit; 4 hours is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires measuring, sawing lumber, nailing/screwing in varied outdoor/workshop settings, multi-step planning, and tool switching (saws, drills, levels). Well beyond 2026 humanoid dexterity, tool-use generalization, and unstructured environment capability.

task: Load and unload baggage in baggage compartments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = loading/unloading baggage for a single aircraft. A typical narrow-body flight has ~100–150 bags; a crew of 2–3 takes ~20–40 min total.
robot_success_rationale: Bags vary hugely in size, weight, and shape; compartment is confined and awkward; outdoor ramp environment is unstructured; robot must lift heavy bags overhead into bins. Dexterity, confined-space mobility, and generalization all limit success significantly.

task: Test and measure patient's strength, motor development and function, sensory perception, functional capacity, or respiratory or circulatory efficiency and record data.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a full clinical assessment session for one patient, covering multiple domains (strength, motor, sensory, etc.); typically 30–60 min for a therapist or clinician.
robot_success_rationale: Requires clinical judgment, nuanced patient communication, interpreting ambiguous responses, adaptive test administration, and safe physical interaction with a vulnerable person — far beyond current humanoid capability in unstructured caregiving contexts.

task: Operate zoom lenses, changing images according to specifications and rehearsal instructions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = executing a single zoom lens adjustment per specification/cue during a shoot or live production. A skilled camera operator completes each adjustment in seconds to a few minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise fine-motor control of camera zoom rings or servo controls, real-time visual/compositional judgment, and following nuanced rehearsal cues. Current humanoids lack the fine dexterity, camera-operation tooling, and cinematographic scene understanding needed.

task: Examine patients to obtain information about their physical condition.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single patient physical examination (history, vitals, auscultation, palpation, assessment). Competent clinician typically takes 15–25 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires nuanced sensory perception (auscultation, palpation), clinical judgment, patient communication, and fine dexterity. Current humanoids lack medical-grade sensors, diagnostic reasoning, and safe patient-contact protocols.

task: Conduct electrocardiogram (EKG), phonocardiogram, echocardiogram, stress testing, or other cardiovascular tests to record patients' cardiac activity, using specialized electronic test equipment, recording devices, or laboratory instruments.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient cardiovascular test session. A standard 12-lead EKG takes ~10 min; an echocardiogram or stress test takes 30–60 min. Median across test types ≈ 45 min.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise electrode/probe placement on a human patient, nuanced patient interaction/communication, clinical judgment, and operation of specialized medical devices. Current humanoids lack medical-grade dexterity, safe patient-contact protocols, and regulatory clearance for autonomous clinical procedures.

task: Perform a variety of duties as requested by client, such as obtaining household supplies or running errands.
human_time_rationale: One errand instance (e.g., fetching household supplies from a store or nearby location); median travel + task completion for a competent household worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor navigation, vehicle operation or public transit, unstructured store environments, and adaptive decision-making across highly varied tasks. Current humanoids cannot drive or reliably navigate unpredictable public spaces autonomously.

task: Transport patients to and from treatment areas, using wheelchairs or providing standing support.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one patient transport trip (e.g., room to treatment area and back). Typical trip involves positioning, transferring, navigating hallways, and returning — ~10 minutes median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires safe patient contact, dynamic standing support, wheelchair maneuvering in cluttered hospital corridors, and adaptive social interaction. Current humanoids lack safe compliant patient handling, real-world hospital navigation reliability, and clinical trust/certification.

task: Clean and drive funeral vehicles, such as cars or hearses, in funeral processions.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning one funeral vehicle (hearse or car). A competent worker washes, wipes, and polishes interior/exterior in ~25–35 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: General cleaning (wiping, mopping) is achievable at ~40–70%, but vehicle exterior/interior cleaning requires navigating around a large object, reaching all panels, and using cloths/tools precisely — reducing success notably.

task: Bolt component parts together to form tower assemblies, using hand tools.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one tower section by bolting component parts together. A competent worker typically takes 30–60 min per section using hand tools.
robot_success_rationale: Bolting structural tower parts requires high-torque tool use, precise alignment of heavy/large components, and two-handed coordination. Current humanoids lack the grip force, tool-handling dexterity, and spatial reasoning for reliable autonomous bolting of large structural assemblies.

task: Fill processing or cooking containers, such as kettles, rotating cookers, pressure cookers, or vats, with ingredients, by opening valves, by starting pumps or injectors, or by hand.
human_time_rationale: One instance = filling one cooking container (kettle, vat, cooker) — gathering ingredients, operating valves/pumps, or hand-loading; typically 5–10 min per fill cycle.
robot_success_rationale: Valve-turning and pump activation require precise tool-use and force control; hand-loading varied ingredients into large vats involves heavy lifting and unstructured manipulation. Perception of fill levels and generalizing across container types is challenging for current humanoids.

task: Drive vehicles to or from workshops or customers' workplaces or homes.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one vehicle trip between a workshop/customer location. Typical local trip is ~15–35 min depending on distance and traffic; 25 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Humanoid robots cannot operate passenger vehicles in traffic. L4 AVs exist but are narrow-route systems, not general-purpose robots. No current frontier humanoid can physically drive a car autonomously on public roads.

task: Maintain or repair agricultural facilities, equipment, or tools to ensure operational readiness, safety, and cleanliness.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single maintenance/repair session on one piece of equipment or facility area. A competent farmhand typically spends ~1 hour per session on inspection, cleaning, lubrication, or minor repairs.
robot_success_rationale: Agricultural maintenance spans highly varied, unstructured environments and tasks — diagnosing faults, using wrenches/grease guns, operating heavy machinery, working outdoors on rough terrain. Current humanoids lack the generalization, tool dexterity, and domain knowledge required.

task: Cut, trim and shape hair or hairpieces, based on customers' instructions, hair type, and facial features, using clippers, scissors, trimmers and razors.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one full haircut/style session for a single customer. A competent barber/stylist typically spends 20–45 minutes per client.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity with sharp tools near a moving human head, real-time aesthetic judgment, varied hair types, and safe human contact — far beyond current humanoid manipulation and safety capabilities.

task: Operate cash registers to receive payments from patrons.
human_time_rationale: One instance = processing a single patron's payment transaction: scanning/entering items, accepting payment, making change, issuing receipt. Typical retail checkout takes ~1–3 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (handling cash, coins, cards, receipts), reliable UI interaction with varied POS terminals, social/verbal exchange with patrons, and error recovery. Current humanoids lack the precision, generalization, and social fluency for autonomous cashiering.

task: Provide a variety of materials and resources for children to explore, manipulate, and use, both in learning activities and in imaginative play.
human_time_rationale: One instance = setting up and distributing a variety of materials/resources for one activity session. A competent educator takes ~20 min to gather, arrange, and present materials.
robot_success_rationale: Requires contextual judgment about age-appropriateness, safety, developmental goals, and imaginative play value. Current robots lack the cognitive and social reasoning to curate and meaningfully present diverse, purpose-matched materials for children.

task: Perform fire protection or suppression duties, such as constructing fire breaks or disposing of brush.
human_time_rationale: One instance = constructing a fire break or clearing a brush pile in a single session; a competent wildland firefighter typically completes a meaningful segment in ~2 hours.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor rough terrain navigation, heavy tool use (chainsaws, pulaskis), brush handling, and situational judgment in dynamic fire environments. Current humanoids lack the mobility, strength, endurance, and fire-safety tolerance for autonomous wildland fire work.

task: Monitor functioning of equipment and make necessary modifications to ensure system operates in conformance with specifications.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single monitoring round plus any adjustments. A competent technician typically spends ~30 min checking equipment status, reading gauges/displays, diagnosing deviations, and making modifications.
robot_success_rationale: Requires reading diverse gauges/panels, diagnosing deviations, and making precise control adjustments. Robots lack generalized fault diagnosis, contextual judgment, and the dexterity to operate arbitrary industrial controls reliably in 2026.

task: Design and fabricate dental prostheses, or supervise dental technicians and laboratory bench workers who construct the devices.
human_time_rationale: One instance = designing and fabricating a single dental prosthesis (e.g., a crown or denture). A skilled dental lab technician typically spends 3–5 hours per unit across design, casting, finishing, and QC steps.
robot_success_rationale: Requires extreme fine dexterity (sub-millimeter dental work), specialized lab tools (wax carving, porcelain firing, CAD/CAM systems), expert material judgment, and supervisory/cognitive oversight — far beyond current humanoid robot capabilities.

task: Use complementary aids, such as infrared lamps, wet compresses, ice, and whirlpool baths to promote clients' recovery, relaxation, and well-being.
human_time_rationale: One instance = applying a single complementary therapy session (e.g., infrared lamp or compress) to one client; includes setup, application, monitoring, and removal.
robot_success_rationale: Requires delicate clinical judgment, safe patient handling, temperature/pain feedback interpretation, and adaptive interaction with a human body. Current robots lack reliable tactile sensing, therapeutic safety protocols, and the social/clinical judgment needed.

task: Investigate crop, property, or habitat damage or destruction or instances of water pollution to determine causes and to advise property owners of preventive measures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = a single damage investigation visit: traveling to site, assessing crop/property/habitat/water damage, identifying cause, and advising owner. Typically 1–3 hours for a competent field agent.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied outdoor/rural terrain, multi-modal damage assessment (visual, chemical, ecological), expert reasoning about causation, and verbal advisory communication with a property owner — well beyond 2026 frontier robot capabilities.

task: Perform tie spacing layout and measure, mark, drill or cut.
human_time_rationale: One instance = laying out, measuring, marking, and drilling/cutting tie spacing on a single structural member or panel. A competent worker typically takes 15–25 minutes per unit.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise measurement, marking, and drilling/cutting on varied workpieces. Current humanoids lack the fine-motor accuracy for consistent layout marking and safe tool operation (drill/saw) in unstructured settings, especially with measuring tape and marking tools.

task: Check for and remove moisture or contamination in damaged areas and keep areas dry until repairs are complete.
human_time_rationale: One instance = checking and drying one damaged area. Involves inspecting, wiping, applying drying measures, and briefly monitoring. Typical for a construction/restoration worker.
robot_success_rationale: Requires moisture detection via sensors/tools, targeted drying with equipment (fans, towels), and sustained monitoring in unstructured, possibly hazardous damaged environments. Poor tool-use generalization and situational judgment limit success.

task: Cut and peel lead sheathing and insulation from defective or newly installed cables and conduits prior to splicing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cutting and peeling sheathing/insulation from one cable end or splice point. A skilled electrician/cable splicer typically takes 10–20 minutes per splice prep.
robot_success_rationale: Requires precise force-controlled cutting of lead/insulation without nicking conductors — extremely fine dexterity. Varied cable diameters, materials, and confined conduit environments push current humanoid capability far beyond demonstrated limits.

task: Clean and polish furniture and fixtures.
human_time_rationale: One instance = cleaning and polishing a typical set of furniture/fixtures in one room (e.g., desk, chairs, shelving, lamps). A competent cleaner takes ~20–30 min.
robot_success_rationale: Robots can wipe flat surfaces but struggle with varied furniture shapes, polishing pressure control, reaching tight spots, handling delicate fixtures, and applying/buffing polish evenly. Generalization across furniture types is still weak.

task: Replenish supplies, such as drinking glasses, linens, writing supplies, and bathroom items.
human_time_rationale: One instance = restocking a single guest room or suite with all standard supplies (glasses, linens, toiletries, writing materials). A competent housekeeper typically completes this in 10–20 minutes.
robot_success_rationale: Requires navigating varied room layouts, identifying and placing diverse item types (soft linens, fragile glass, small toiletries), and fine placement in cupboards/shelves. Current humanoids lack reliable generalization across all these item categories and precise placement in unstructured hotel environments.

task: Prune or trim trees, shrubs, or hedges, using shears, pruners, or chain saws.
human_time_rationale: One instance = pruning/trimming a single tree, shrub, or hedge section. A competent groundskeeper typically spends 20–60 min depending on size and complexity; 45 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires outdoor uneven terrain navigation, identifying correct branches to cut (horticultural judgment), wielding heavy/vibrating tools (shears, chain saw) safely, and working at height — all well beyond 2026 general-purpose humanoid capabilities.

task: Assemble prototypes or models of circuits, instruments, and systems for use in testing.
human_time_rationale: One instance = assembling one prototype circuit/instrument/system. A competent electronics technician typically takes 1–3 hours depending on complexity; 120 min is a reasonable median.
robot_success_rationale: Requires fine dexterity (component placement, soldering, wire routing), schematic interpretation, and adaptive problem-solving. Current humanoids lack the precision, tool-use (soldering iron, multimeter), and cognitive planning needed for prototype electronics assembly.

task: Repair, fabricate, and install steel or wood fittings, using blueprints, shop sketches, and instruction manuals.
human_time_rationale: One instance = one fitting job (read blueprints, fabricate/repair, and install a single steel or wood fitting). A skilled tradesperson typically takes 60–120 min end-to-end.
robot_success_rationale: Requires interpreting complex 2D/3D blueprints, operating grinders, saws, welders, and drills, precise multi-step fabrication, and fine positional fitting. Current humanoids lack tool-use generalization, blueprint reading, and the dexterity for reliable fabrication and installation at trade quality.
